title: Creating Diffusion Tensor Images (DTI)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"an2tq","text":"This procedure describes the steps required to create diffusion tensor images (DTI) from data from the MRI scanner. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cg4pr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fj8au","text":"The following programs are required to go through the protocol:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":63}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8h5if","text":"MRtrix3 ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a3q7f","text":"TORTOISE","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4ldqh","text":"ITKsnap","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4kluo","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ed37j","text":"Note: If you are working from a Windows computer, you will need to follow X2Go Client Set-Up (dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.6tvhen6) before you can begin this protocol. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":94,"length":40}],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"D02449F7BFD840769412E305C7C36842","url":"https://dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.6tvhen6"}}}}
steps: Open a new terminal.
Create a directory to place data. (If not already created.) mkdir mkdir ~/Data/Projects/ Create directory. 0-58-generic #64-Ubuntu SMP
Go to the directory that you plan to use. cd cd Go to a specified directory. 0-58-generic #64-Ubuntu SMP

Copy directory where your raw data files that will be acquistioned, reside in. You will need the following file types: Bvals Bvecs data.img data.hdr* - ( may not need this in all cases - used for FOV origin)

Find where the files you plan to copy are located. Make sure to use the original root i.e /home/... as you will get an error if you start from another location. ls ls To list files in a specific directory. 0-58-generic #64-Ubuntu SMP

Copy the specific files that you need. Alternatively, you can use . to specify the current directory you are in. cp cp Copy file or entire directory to a specified location. 0-58-generic #64-Ubuntu SMP
Perform conversion between different file types. mrconvert mrconvert MRtrix3
Import data from nifti format to TORTOISE list file format. A new directory will be created and the folder will be labeled ____proc . Example: ImportNIFTI ImportNIFTI TORTOISE
Go to the new directory that was created from the previous step.

Correct motion. Use DIFFPREP command. ( This is a command from TOROISE ). Note: This step is time & core consuming. Also, this step removes Gibbs ringing and noise DIFFPREP DIFFPREP Corrects motion. TORTOISE
Open one of the populated files using ITKsnap.
Within ITKsnap, select Active Contour (aka Snake) Segmentation Mode from the toolbar.
A new side window will appear. Select Segment 3D .

Toggle the thresholds (lower & upper) so that the blue mask covers the region you want repelled. ( Regions that are empty space or not the brain of interest should have this mask ). Note: Try not to change the defaults.
When the thresholds have been finalized, select Next .

Set the cursor in the relative center of your object of interest. Select Add bubble at cursor . A red circle(seed/bubble) will be placed at the location. Note: You may need to repeat this if you have multiple objects of interest.
When the bubbles have been set and you have no more to add, select Next .

Press the play buton to have the seed grow within the mask that was set-up in a previous step. Note: Red should propagate and fill the entire region(s) of interest. This may require viewing the images from the different planes to confirm.
Once the region(s) of interest are completely filled, select Finish.
Press Update in the lower left corner of the ITKsnap interface to view the new 3D segmentation image.

Select Segmentation and Save As from the drop-down menu. You will be prompted to save your file as a __.nii.gz file type. It is recommended you name your file __mask to clarify segmentation has already been performed.
Confirm what directory you are currently in. pwd pwd 0-58-generic #64-Ubuntu SMP
Close out of ITKsnap.

Estimate diffusion tensor and Bo amplitude. EstimateTensorNLLS EstimateTensorNLLS -i ___DTI_DMC.list -m ___mask.nii Generates diffusion tensor and B0 amplitude. TORTOISE This command will generate 2 files: ___N1_DT.nii (diffusion tensor) ___DT_AM.nii (B0 amplitude)
In an open terminal, unzip the ___.nii.gz file that was segmented in the previous step. gunzip gunzip ___.nii.gz Unzip file(s) of interest.
Compute all tensor maps on the ___N1_DTI.nii output file from the previous step. ComputeAllTensorMaps.bash ComputeAllTensorMaps.bash ___N1_DT.nii Computer all tensor maps. TORTOISE
Open the ___DEC.nii output file in ITKsnap to view the primary eigenvector map.
Within ITKsnap, select Layer Inspector .
From the Layer Inspector window, select the Display mode drop-down menu and select RGB Display .

The final result will show a colorized image of your region of interest that allows a visual guide that estimates location, orientation, and anisotropy of the brain's white matter tracts based on the diffusion of water molecules.
Press Update in the lower left corner of the ITKsnap interface to view the image in the new display mode.
creator: Laurel Dieckhaus
